topic: Arrays - std::array::from_ref and from_mut for Single-Element Slices

turns.content: I'm trying to automate a log rotation script where I need to pass a single String into a function that expects a &str slice, but the API seems to require &[T]. Can you show me how std::array::from_ref helps with this without copying data?

You can use std::array::from_ref on your single String value to get a &[T; 1] where T is str. Since strings are slices internally, this creates a zero-copy view that the function can consume as a &[str]. The result is already coercible to &[][T] if needed, so you don't need extra conversion steps.

That sounds efficient but I'm worried about memory safety since I'm creating a new array. Does std::array::from_mut apply here too? What's the difference between from_ref and from_mut in this context?

std::array::from_mut is for mutable references when you need to mutate that single element inside your function. It produces &mut [T; 1] instead of &[T; 1]. The key distinction is that from_ref takes an immutable reference while from_mut requires a mutable one, which matters if your log rotation logic needs to append directly to the stored string.
I see, but what about slice::from_ref? I read somewhere they produce identical results. Should I avoid using std::array for this?

slice::from_ref does indeed return &[T; 1] for a single element just like std::array::from_ref, so functionally they are equivalent in most cases. However, std::array is the more idiomatic path when you specifically need array semantics, whereas slice::from_ref might be chosen if you're already working within the slice ecosystem and want consistency.

Okay so I should just grab my String, call from_ref on it, then pass that to my function. One last thing though - does this work with owned Strings or do I need Arc? What about passing a single Vec instead?

This works directly with owned Strings since they are already slices. For Vectors you would use std::array::from_ref on the first element if you only care about that one item, but for full vector semantics you typically pass &mut [T] or &[T] directly without wrapping in an array unless your function specifically requires a fixed-size array type.

Ah! So I can avoid copying my entire log entry just by using this pattern. One final question - does the compiler automatically coerce from &[str; 1] to &str when needed, or do I need explicit casting?

The coercion happens at compile time for single-element arrays in most contexts where a reference is expected, but explicit casting with as_ref() is safer if your code reaches an ambiguous state. This ensures the type system knows exactly what you're passing without relying on implicit conversions that might break in edge cases.
